speaker: interviewer
text: --a minute ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: A minute ago I said that we were a very close-knit family. My aunts, my uncles, cousins, we were so close. And that's what hurt more than anything else. Because we were visiting each other. They lived in Brody. We lived in Radziwillów, what that time, we used to travel by horse and buggy. We visit them on the weekends. And this nightmare is going to stay with me as long as I live. I think I wouldn't say I-- every day. But most, all the time, most of the time I think about it, what was happening. It's always going to be in my mind, what happened during the war. And just think about it, I didn't have it the worst yet. I still-- we were still lucky compared to other people that, what-- what they went through. So we were still fortunate. And with all this fortune, to go through all this emotionally, it's very difficult. And so that's all I wanted to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's now go back to where you-- you were-- you were liberated. You say the Russian liberated you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: --they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the woods at the time that they went through there, yeah. We were in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you-- from liberation, you went to Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then a Displaced Persons camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: By Furth, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I wanted to ask you what-- what did people do in the DP camps? Your father, your mother, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I-- we didn't do very much there. Because we were just waiting to be-- to-- to go to either to United States. That was like a transient camp, or to Israel, or wherever we would get a visa to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like a waiting period. I remember taking some courses in mechanics they used to have. And I didn't stay very long in the DP camp because I moved to Munich, like I told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there maybe less than a year. And I didn't do very much there, then. We just didn't do a heck of a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you exist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they supplied us with food there. We had all the food we wanted. I mean, that was no problem. And we used to go out into town with my friends. Used to go to movies, to theater, and all that stuff. So we had a pretty good life. My friends still live in Buffalo here, and I visit here-- I just visited one of them. And we were together, and we were enjoying it together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to find out what happened to the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, we tried. And my uncle, he was very energetic-- he was in the Bricha, too. And he tried to connections. And he couldn't figure out exactly what happened. Nobody could tell what happened to the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They just-- got-- got killed, were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never been back, no. My brother was, but I wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right. He said it's still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Radziwillów too?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Brody is there, where I was born. I was born in Brody. Brody is there too, yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Probably not very many Jews over there now.

speaker: interviewee
text: He found one Jew in the whole city. He went to the city hall, he said. And there was not in the records. But one girl heard that he looked for Jewish people. Then she said, I know one Jewish guy that-- and he used to-- he happened to know my father. He was working with my father, the-- the Jew. And he went to visit him. And he talked to him. He told me all about him. No, he's like, assimilated. Completely assimilated now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you come to the United States then?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- we came in April, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: From

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat. By boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: From?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled from Munich to Hamburg. And then we took-- from the port, we took the boat. And we came on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother, your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- all together again, we stayed together all the time. And we came together from Hamburg. I think we came to Boston, if I am not mistaken. But I remember going by train through New York. And our destination was Akron. Because we were sponsored by-- my father's second cousin was Mr Buchsbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember Buchsbaum company? You know, rubber mat company here? He was my father's second cousin. He sponsored us. You had to have a sponsor to come to the United States. Now, did you come to New York or Boston?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we came to Akron. We went by New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you came by boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat. We went to Boston, I think. I think it was Boston. I can't remember now, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you were feeling when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or New York, maybe? Oh, freedom. Number one was freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was different lifestyle they didn't feel any antisemitism. I just-- and I felt like I was in paradise when I came here. It was just a different-- people are not selfish here. They were so good to you. Everybody was nice. And over there, between the Germans, so selfish, everybody for himself. Not the Jews so much, but the Germans. And you lived amongst them. And here Americans were so nice and pleasant. And it was just a different-- like-- like I was in the paradise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the food was plentiful, no comparison. Over there was-- still, after the war, they didn't have much food. And here we had plenty of food. you know? It was-- it was a different world, really. You like to watch movies when you were in Poland. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --then you came to the United States. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was it what you-- what you guessed it might be, coming to the United States? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't-- didn't give it too much thought. We had some relatives in New York, I know they wrote letters and-- to us. And my parents usually, they used to read them. I didn't read them. They were in Yiddish and I didn't read them too much. But they knew more than I did what was going on. I just heard a lot about United States and the freedom and all that. And I just-- I was overwhelmed when I came here. It was more than I expected.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got off the boat, where were you, in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The boat, we went through Customs, I remember. Big-- I remember big hallway and with Customs, and they were checking the baggage and everything. That's all I remember now.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was almost, what, 50 years ago? 49? No, a little less than 50 years ago. You don't remember that much detail. I-- I think I don't-- About how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949? I was born in '28. So I was about 21 years old when I came here, 21 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you seek employment then, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Well, we came to Akron. They put us up in the hotel. Mr. Buchsbaum put us up in the hotel. He came and he-- his shop was here in Canton. And he used to take my dad. And my dad was an accountant for him here too. And then about two weeks later, he brought us all to Canton. We stayed in a hotel until they found a house for us. We didn't know what-- how to go about it. And then I got a job there as a laborer in the factory there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did all everything there that was-- that they found out that I was very machine shop work, and they put me in the machine shop there. And I was doing some work there, in the machine shop. So I was in the labor gang, and I was in the machine shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, I went and took some courses, technical course-- I had one year of college there. And I went to Timken Vocational School, I remember, took some English.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's where you learned English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a little bit there. A little bit I took on my own. And then I took some blueprint-reading courses. All-- I was-- I wanted to be an engineer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was taking all these technical courses that I-- were leading up to it. What was in 1950, '51. And then at night, after that, I enrolled in Akron U and I went there for a year, and then Ohio State, where I graduated. When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in 19-- 1957, Thanksgiving. And I met her here in Canton. I called her up. Somebody told-- gave me her number. I called her up and I made a date. And that's how I met her. And then she was living in Cleveland then. And I remember that first Sunday. She said, I have to go back home. I said, I'll take you back home to Cleveland, back to Cleveland. Then I took her to Cleveland. And then we made another date for the following week, and so on and on. And she used to come there. Her parents lived in Canton. And we dated. And then in 1958, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell her your experience soon--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soon after you started dating? Did she know--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, she knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she knew all about me. Yeah, she knew all about us. Because her friend was-- maybe you remember, [? Brigita's. ?] You remember them? No. That was their friends, and they knew about us. And they told her about me, you know, what. Yeah, they did. She knew about me, that I-- she thought I was in a concentration camp. And then I told her that I was in the forest and the woods, everything, farms--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she had heard about the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she knew then, yeah. There were movies then, television and everything, about the camps. You know, I remember watching it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard for her to believe, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: She still does not believe it. I tell you, we went to that lecture, that lady that wrote that book, And she said what she lived-- she told us what she lived through. And then she-- we're reading the book bow. I got the book from her. It's impossible. She said, you couldn't go through all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fought over the dead, you know? She was in that prison then, she was dead already. And some Jewish guy's father took her in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is why she's alive now. And it-- it's hard for me too, to believe it, and no wonder. But she-- she says, I just can't believe it, that this happened. I just can't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I told her, you want it that other people don't believe it? And my-- my own wife. She said-- if she comes in, you'll ask her and she'll tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's amazing. You know, it's difficult for me even, to believe what was happening in Auschwitz there, with so many accounts, what I read about and I tell you, I wasn't there. But I heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you read about what happened now, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I cry. It cry, my heart cries. I think of my relatives, that they were there, They died there in the ovens. That-- that's what I think about. I don't know how they died. [CRYING] [SOBBING] They were so close. I-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But now, again--

speaker: interviewer
text: Take-- take your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: This--

speaker: interviewer
text: It is hard to understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I remember them so well. I wasn't that young when the war started. I was already 10, 12 years old. So I remember all the details. They were so good to me. We were so close too. That's the problem. I try not to get too close to the family anymore now. It isn't so healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because when you pass away, something, people take it too hard. I just-- I don't know. I don't want them to get too attached. That's why--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, passing away is one thing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And being-- being murdered is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my folks passed away and we so much-- when they died--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, after going through so much together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went together, so much through, together. They were so devoted. And they're ready to give their lives away for us. That's more than an average. Parents die. Of course, we expect that. But this was more than that. I'm sorry to say that we were so close all together. They gave their lives away for us. They would have given their life away for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was tough. But you know, time heals a lot of things. I don't dwell on it. I know that we all have to die sometimes. And I do not dwell on it, because we had happy years together. And that's what I think about. But still, it's-- when you're closer together, closer to death, it's harder to take. Yeah. What were your occupations since you came to United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I started here like I told you, the labor gang there in the rubber factory. Then I went-- well, I became a machine operator, machinist. After that, I-- before I went to college here, I remember I went to Detroit. My uncle lived in Detroit. Stayed with him. And I got a little job in a private-- because I was an engineer, I got a draftsman-- became a draftsman for him. I wasn't doing so well. Because I didn't have enough training in that. So the job didn't work out too well. Lucky for me, I came back to Canton. I didn't want to look for another job. And that's when I went and enrolled in Akron U. Enrolled-- enrolled to Akron U in Engineering. And I had a heck of a time because I didn't know English. So it was very difficult. I-- the only course I flunked was the English course. Because I couldn't speak English. But the second time I took it, I got a B. And that gave me encouragement. I got better. I understood it better. I worked harder. And that's when things started to get easier. So I went here for about less than a year. And then I went to Ohio State for another five years, where I graduated in the Mechanical Engineering. And then I worked for the Timken Company when I graduated, after I graduated. Actually, in the summers I used to do surveying work for the Timken Company. In the summers they used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Civil engineer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Civil engineering, surveying, surveying their plots. They had a lot of land. So me and another fellow named Carl Garafalo, we became very good friends. He was a wonderful guy. Maybe you heard of him, Carl Garafalo. And he was with me for 20 years. We worked for the Timken Company. He had three degrees, that fellow. He was Very educated. And we had a lot in common. And I worked in the design department, designing equipment to produce steel. For 20 years, I worked for them. And it was a wonderful opportunity. They were-- they were wonderful to work for. They were very fair to us. There were about five other Jewish engineers working all over the plant there.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Timken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some famous one was very famous. Fleischman, maybe you heard of him. He was-- he developed Krupp steel. You've heard of Krupp steel? He was one of the developers, original developers. And I used to know him. And he worked for the Timken company in the metallurgical department.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had some what-- Mr. Wechstein. Did you hear of Wechstein? He was a chief engineer in the [? Bering ?] factory here. And he-- I knew him. And then we had a few more Jewish people working there. They were wonderful to us, all the way. Did you know other-- in Canton, Ohio here, did you know of other survivors then? And did you have contact with them? Other people who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got to know your father, was one of them. The Goldmans was another one that I met here. [INAUDIBLE], you heard of [INAUDIBLE]? There was another.

speaker: interviewer
text: And eventually, we got to know more and more. But that's-- the [? Rakowskis. ?] They-- you remember, Ron's parents? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were close together, yeah. we were-- meeting sometimes and talking to each other, yes. We were very close with your mom. I remember your mom came here. And we were pretty close with your parents too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel-- did-- did you feel like you wanted to tell your story then, to other Jew-- other American Jews or other Americans? Or did you-- or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really did not, except one exception. I-- I Well to tell it to my kids. I wanted them to know what was happening. But I did not want to talk to anybody else about it. I really didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just didn't feel like it, I don't know. Just didn't want to. I just didn't want to. I don't-- I can't explain why I didn't want to. It-- it-- it affects

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think they didn't want to know? Or you didn't want to tell? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think people cared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I still don't think. I think the world is against it, still yet, even now. As generally speaking. I think the whole world is against us. Our only hope is Israel. There's no other question about it in my mind. I am not patriot. I'm not nationalistic. I'm just telling you from experience, we have no other choice. The Jewish people, they only want their own land. That's the only way that we can survive. Otherwise it's-- it's-- it's problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think something like that could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a possibility. Look what happened. Germany was excellent, was wonderful to the Jewish people, excellent. And look what happened, in 19-- when Hitler came to power. He poisoned most of the Germans. And that's what happened, the worst country in the whole world, for the Jews. And it was the-- one of the best, best democracies. Funny thing happens now. Because I go to shul. In Germany, they used to have the prayer for our country. Jews, when you live in the country, become a citizen of the country. And you're part of it. And it was a prayer for our country.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, when I say that prayer for our country, even now I think about all the non-Jews that are again-- I'm sorry I'm telling you my inner feelings now. Is it worth saying that for our-- is it our country? I don't know anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm getting senile. I don't trust people as much as I used to. You know, it's-- it's difficult. When you go to a lot like that, there are a very few people that I would trust that are non-Jewish. One of them was Garafalo. He was just a wonderful father, some wonderful people here. But you don't know who to trust anymore. When you saw Germans turns over-- turn over in the night, they were your friends. Or Ukrainians. I forgot to tell you that, one other story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in-- when we lived in Brody, when the Germans took over, we-- my-- we lived like, in a duplex, or a triplex even, there was another family. There was-- there was a couple, they are a Polish couple. And we lived in one section of the house, the Polish couple, and then his parents lived in another section of the house. It was divided. It was not like a duplex here, it was divided house. So we lived there. One-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --evening, or in the daytime, my mom heard screaming, and a woman screaming next door. It was like the way that by 10 doors, that you heard scream. And she ran over there. And she says, my son is dying. He's dying. Son was my age. You know, there was no taxis, there was no nothing. My mom ran to the hospital and brought a doctor by force, I don't know for what. And he came, and he came with a horse and buggy and took the boy to the hospital and saved his life. The woman came to my mom, she kissed her. I remember that. And she said, as long as I live, my life is yours. I-- you saved my son. My son would have died. He had high fever. His father, same thing. Came, kissed my mom's hand. They were polite, everything. He was Ukrainian. She was Polish. When the Germans came, he became part of the SS. And he was in the big building there, where the SS men were. They took my father to a labor battalion. And he couldn't physically work very well. My mom ran to the Gestapo there, to the SS building. She ran to him and he threw her out. He said, don't you dare come to me again. That guy, that she saved his son's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was so good to us in all that. How can you trust people after that? You tell me how.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I see people come in, that they are nice to me and all that, I want to trust them. I really do. I honestly, sincerely, want to trust people. . There are a few people that are wonderful and great. I don't have the same trust I used to have for the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: This'll never going to change. I don't think it'll change with me. You what what I mean? That's my inner feelings. And I'm telling you, from in the middle of the heart. But don't you think he was trying to maybe save himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's possible he tried. But when he came home, he could have at least come in, and we still lived together there. He didn't want anything to do with the Jews. He was afraid, I guess. Well, still, it happened. That's in my mind. This one point is in my mind. I-- I'm working here. I'm right now, in the printing business. I have my print shop. And I meet-- many people come in. And some are wonderful people. And they mean well. And I look at them, and I see the two faces in them, so I can't help it. See, that guy that I knew, that's got awful, I knew him for so many years. I knew that he was a friend, a real friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm sure majority of people are nice and good. They're good. But how do I know who is and who isn't? I don't know. You can't enter their hearts. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that's-- that's the dilemma that I have to live with. And I hope my kids don't have to live with that. But I want them to know the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about your kids. When were they born? And give me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My one son, the younger, the older son is Dennis. And he was born in 1961, July, 7-11, '61. That's a lucky number. The other side is named Gordon. And he was born in 1964, January 21st. And I am just very blessed with very wonderful kids. They're just-- just the greatest. [CRYING] [SIGHS] See, I got emotional. I can't get off of it. They're just the greatest kids in the world. They'd do anything for me. I think Gordon saved my life. I was-- I had a heart attack. I was on vacation. I was in the no-man's country. There was no hospitals. He happened to work here, and he-- he is in medicine. He's a doctor. And he came flying right to me, right away. Left his job. And he tries to-- he ran to the plane and transferred me to San Diego, to the Mercy Hospital, where he knew the surgeons, and they operated on me, and they treated me wonderfully.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe he saved my life. That's what I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incredible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was incredible. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't ask for any better--

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of your story do they know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They know a lot of it. They know what was happening, generally speaking. I didn't get into too many details, like I did now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell them when they were very young? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, let me think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask you about what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. They-- first of all, Dennis didn't want to talk about it. That was funny. So I didn't force it on them. Said that was bygones, that we don't want to hear bad news and all that. I said, OK, Dennis. I just wanted-- then when they got older, and say, I do want you to know about it. Because you should know that what was happening. And then he listened. He got old and he listened to me. And Gordon is very interested in listening to all the details. I don't want to burden them too much. Actually, he wanted to go with me to that town, when I was born, and wanted to see it. And then the things started, with Yugoslavia, with the Bosnian-- the unrest, and the Russia, it changed from communist, you know. And it just-- the unrest, and there I-- we were afraid to go there, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't go. But he was more-- Gordon is more understanding, I think, than Dennis. Dennis just didn't want to hear much about it. But I told them basically, what went on, so they-- they should know. Here it is now, it's 50 years-- more than 50 years after the end of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any-- any message or-- or lesson that you would like to tell your children and grandchildren, about what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think that one of the most important things on my mind is to have your own country, no matter what. You have to have your own country. I don't think this would happen, that, badly as it did if we had Israel then. And this is our only hope. For the Jew, it's our only hope. Because we are really not wanted. It was proven that we were not wanted by the world. There were ships coming to Cuba. Did you hear about that ship that came to Cuba, they rejected it and sent it back to Germany and to-- to the ovens, said they wouldn't accept the Jews. Americans, Roosevelt had a rough time. He-- he-- maybe he wanted to accept some Jews. But the nation, as a whole, didn't want to accept Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are not really wanted. And that's the only hope, is our own country. That's the only place that you can feel safe, regardless of whether there is unrest, unrest everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: But still this is, I think, the most important thing for a Jew, in my opinion. I can't tell you anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- well, why do you-- why do you think you-- you survived all this? How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's pure luck. It was just pure luck. When I told you about the German coming out to my hiding place. And I know he wasn't stupid. He knew I was there. He had to know. There is no way that he would be that-- that stupid, because the hole is there, and just go under the wall. We were there in the other section, if you can understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was pure luck, I think, that he hollered down there to the other guys, there's nobody here. We can go. He could've just said, come and look for people here, hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew-- the hiding place was given out. Somebody gave him the hiding place. They knew exactly where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think they found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the neighbors. We were there for about-- I don't know how long. I can't remember. I don't want to say how long-- quite a few months. And of course, we all-- dad used to go out to get food. They saw him, at night, go out. They must have seen him. And they know that there were Jews there, the two families.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave them exactly the hiding place. They knew where we were and everything, exactly, where we were. Because where we were hiding, that hole in the kitchen, they had one-room apartment there. With a kitchen floor-- there was a kitchen and dining room.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put a-- a piece of furniture over top of it so you couldn't-- it's masking, you couldn't see the hole. When the German came, I remember he tried to get the piece of furniture right off. Because somebody told him-- must have told him where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he crawled down into the hole and he said, nobody is there. Now, you figure it out. I don't know, I can't. So isn't that luck? That's pure luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are other escapes that I had that were minor, smaller. They caught us in the woods, some Ukrainians. And we were walking in the woods, and they were walking. They were like, Ukrainian partisans. But they were against the Jews. And we-- we-- as soon as we saw them, we ran. And we knew the woods pretty well too. And they didn't run after us. So that was another. Like, they had their weapons. They didn't shoot after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Close call.

speaker: interviewee
text: Close call again. And luck. That there is nothing more that I can describe than it was lucky that we was-- that we survived. And as a family, yet. That's unusual. But one thing all the-- all the families-- I told you that before, I think-- survived that were in the bunkers with us. All except the two people that got killed. It wasn't because they were Jews. And the Germans were actually afraid or they didn't have enough equipment to come to the woods. Because they were losing the war. So they needed every tank and every soldier sent to the war, I think. And they were afraid for the partisans too. Because they were shooting them. Partisans were shooting them-- at them. And they're killing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't have enough manpower to really send to the woods. Maybe that was another luck-- lucky. Because they could have come and they could have found us right away, where we were. It wasn't that difficult. But they were afraid, OK? I think that's what it was. OK, we're going to take a break. And we're going to continue on.
